column3: - Information security incidents must be reported and managed in a timely and structured manner. - Incident response activities must prioritize safety, containment, and impact reduction. - Evidence related to incidents must be preserved where required. - Lessons learned from incidents must be used to improve security controls. - Incident management practices must support legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations. - Personnel must be able to report security concerns without fear of retaliation.

column3: - The organization must provide a mechanism for confidential or anonymous reporting of security concerns. - Personnel may report suspected incidents, policy violations, or unethical behavior without identifying themselves. - An anonymous reporting channel must be maintained (e.g. anonymous_incident@company.com). - Reports submitted through anonymous channels must be reviewed and handled with the same level of seriousness as identified reports. - Retaliation against individuals who report concerns in good faith is prohibited.

column3: - An information security event is any observable occurrence in a system, network, or service that may be relevant to security. - An information security incident is one or more events that have been assessed and determined to pose a real or potential threat to the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of organizational assets. - All reported events must be reviewed to determine whether they constitute an incident. - Events that do not pose a security risk must be recorded and closed. - Events that indicate compromise, policy violation, or material risk must be escalated and classified as incidents. - Incidents must be classified and prioritized based on potential and actual impact, including: - Impact on confidentiality, integrity, and availability - Scope of affected systems, data, or users - Legal, regulatory, or contractual implications - Potential financial, operational, and reputational cost to the organization - Urgency and likelihood of further impact
